result:
The first thing that comes to me is the sound of a radiator in an old apartment. A metallic, percussive *tink-tink-tink*, like a tiny, impatient ghost trapped in the pipes. It’s a sound that belongs to a specific shade of yellowed wallpaper and the smell of dust settling on baseboard heaters. It’s not my radiator—I haven’t heard one in years—but the memory of it is suddenly, vividly here. That’s the strange tyranny of a blank page: it doesn’t stay blank for long. It fills immediately with the debris of a life, the psychic lint pulled from the pockets of the mind.

I think about the 1000 words not as a limit, but as a container. What do you pour into a container of this size? Not a life story, but an afternoon. Not a manifesto, but a handful of observations, still warm from being held. You could pour in the quiet panic of a Sunday evening, the way the light slants differently, telling you time is moving again after the suspended animation of the weekend. You could pour in the precise, emerald green of a lizard darting over a sun-baked stone, a flash of life so quick and purposeful it makes your own thoughts seem sluggish and gray.

Or you could pour in the question that hums underneath all of this: what is worth saying? In a world of infinite digital chatter, of hot takes and curated lives, what does it mean to simply *write what comes*? It feels like a radical act of trust. To believe that the unfiltered stream—the radiator, the Sunday dread, the green lizard—contains its own coherence. That by following the threads of your own attention, you might stumble upon a pattern that connects to someone else’s humanity. A shared frequency.

I think of my grandmother’s hands. They were cool and dry, a map of rivers and tributaries in blue and brown. When she’d peel apples, the skin would come off in one long, unbroken spiral. She’d let it drop, a delicate red curl on the yellow Formica table. I found that magic more compelling than any fairy tale. There was a skill in it, a lifetime of patience in her fingers that I have not inherited. My apples come off in chunks. I am a chunk-peeler, living in a chunk-peeling world. We lack the time for spirals. We lack the belief that the unbroken spiral matters. But here, in this word-container, I can hold that spiral for a moment. I can give it the 35 words it deserves. See? It just got them.

The window beside me frames a slice of sky and the upper branches of an oak. It is late winter, early spring. The branches are a stark lattice against the gray, but if you look closely, you can see the buds: tiny, clenched fists of potential. They are holding their green secret tightly, waiting for the exact, unerring signal to let go. There’s a profound intelligence in that, a trust in the turning of the world that I envy. I am not good at waiting for signals. I am often prematurely in bud, or stubbornly holding on to last season’s leaves.

This is what comes, you see. Not a single, polished argument, but a collage of sense-impressions and half-remembered things, all vying for a place in the container. The itch of a wool sweater label. The specific loneliness of a single engine drone from a plane you can’t see. The crushing, beautiful weight of all the books you’ll never read. The guilty pleasure of re-watching a familiar movie instead of starting a new one, because you know the shape of its comfort.

Maybe the point of the exercise is to prove that consciousness itself is a kind of writing. We are constantly narrating, editing, associating. The mind is a magpie, stealing shiny bits of the present and tucking them next to treasured fragments of the past. When we “write what comes,” we are simply transcribing the magpie’s hoard. We are acknowledging that the value isn’t necessarily in the singular, priceless jewel, but in the bizarre, glittering heap of it all: the bottle cap next to the silver locket next to the fallen blue jay feather.

And so, the container fills. With the imagined taste of a snowflake from childhood. With the uneasy feeling that we are all becoming worse at being bored, and therefore worse at being truly entertained. With the sudden memory of a friend’s laughter from twenty years ago, a sound I hadn’t consciously recalled until this second, now echoing as clear as the radiator’s *tink*.

I am nearing the end of my allotted space. The awareness of the limit shapes what comes now. I think of final things. The graceful arc of a finished sentence—a period resting on the line like a diver poised at the very end of the board. The quiet after you speak a difficult truth. The way a held breath finally releases. Endings are a kind of gift, too. They tell the magpie, “Enough. Your collection is complete for now. Sit back and look at what you have.”

What I have, here in this container, is not a thesis. It is a record of a mind trying to be faithful to its own moment, on a drizzly afternoon, with a window and an oak tree for witnesses. It is a spiral of apple peel on a Formica table. It is the *tink-tink-tink* of a ghost in the pipes, saying, “I am here. I was here. The heat is on its way.”

And with that, the radiator falls silent. The container is full.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
